coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of carbon quantum-dot films with silicon substrates for improved photovoltaic performances
coredata.dc:creator: Yen, Yu-Cheng
Hsiao, Po-Hsuan
Chen, Shih-Hsiu
Chen, Chia-Yun
coredata.dc:description: Employment of fluorine-ion doped carbon quantum dot (FCQD) films with silicon (Si) substrates is realized via the facile solution process that exhibits the remarkable photovoltaic characterizations. In-depth materials and cell characterizations are performed, and the improvement of charge separation at FCQD/Si interfaces compared with undoped CQD/Si cases are experimentally elucidated, showing the conversion efficiency of 13.6 %, approximately 2 times higher than undoped CQD-based solar cells with conversion efficiency of 6.8%. The results are of great importance for QD-based light-harvesting and optoelectronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Solar energy materials
Silicon
Heterojunction
Surfaces

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Cross-sectional SEM image of FCQD film. (b) Correlated HRTEM image.
Spectral characterizations of FCQDs: (a) EELS analysis, (b) XRD patterns, (c) FTIR spectra and (d) Raman results. The inset in Fig. 1(a) is spatial mapping.
(a) Dark (b) Photovoltaic voltage-current results of FCQD-based devices. (c) Band diagram of undoped CQD/Si (figure left) and doped FCQD/Si (figure right).

head.ce:title: Incorporation of carbon quantum-dot films with silicon substrates for improved photovoltaic performances

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, National Cheng Kung University, Tainan 70101, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Employment of fluorine-ion doped carbon quantum dot (FCQD) films with silicon (Si) substrates is realized via the facile solution process that exhibits the remarkable photovoltaic characterizations. In-depth materials and cell characterizations are performed, and the improvement of charge separation at FCQD/Si interfaces compared with undoped CQD/Si cases are experimentally elucidated, showing the conversion efficiency of 13.6 %, approximately 2 times higher than undoped CQD-based solar cells with conversion efficiency of 6.8%. The results are of great importance for QD-based light-harvesting and optoelectronic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Colloid quantum dots (QDs), the promising materials that exhibit unprecedented photonic, optoelectronic and electronic properties, have been extensively explored for next-generation photovoltaic applications [1]. These can be originated from their compelling advantages such as feasible tunability of band-gap energy for matching solar spectrum, inexpensive solution processability and outstanding light harvesting capability [2]. While the great improvement of QD-based solar cells has been envisioned in recent years, yet the present conversion efficiency is substantially lower than the theoretical Shockley–Queisser efficiency limit of single-junction solar cells. One of critical limitations suppressing the achievable conversion efficiency of QD-based solar cells lies in recombination of photoexcited carriers, which is mainly occurred at defective surfaces of QDs [3,4]. To solve these demanding issues, the adequate passivation procedure is required by either processing the ligand-exchange or doping treatments [5,6]. Although the QDs undergoing the ligand-exchange process can effectively reduce the carrier-recombination probability and charge-transport efficiency due to removing the electrically insulating ligands, the unwanted poor environmental stability and photostability under succeeding solar illuminations limits their practicality. On the other hand, doping treatment can potentially passivate the defective surface of QDs while maintaining the reliability of photovoltaic devices for long-term operation; nevertheless, the gas-involved doping procedures lead the entire synthesis to be lengthy and complicated [7]. Therefore, in this work, the facile and inexpensive solution-processed F-ion doping treatment is employed on carbon QD films, termed as FCQD, which improve their carrier separation characteristics.

Monodispersed FCQDs were synthesized with the conventional acid-assisted ultrasonication [8]. The obtained CQD suspensions are immersed in HF solutions for 1 hr, and then deposited on the monolayer graphene/Si substrates at 100 °C for 1 h in vacuum for uniform adhesion.

Fig. 1 (a) presents the cross-sectional SEM image of fabricated FCQD films, where the uniform layer features with thickness of 72 ± 3 nm can be observed. Moreover, the layer thickness of deposited FCQD films can be adjusted via the deposition cycles, as evidenced in supplemental files. This identifies that the FCQD films functioning as light-absorption constitute can be readily sandwiched with top Ag electrode and underlying Si substrates. Microstructures of a representative FCQD (4 M of HF) are examined with HRTEM image, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The lattice arrangement reveals the zigzag configuration with fringe spacing of 0.31 nm, which is slightly larger than the crystalline spacing of graphite. We speculate that F− doping somewhat distorts the original carbon–carbon spatial arrangement by expending their lattice fringe due to relatively large ion radius of F− (133 pm) compared with C4+ (16 pm).

To get insights into the compositional profiles of F− ions inside FCQDs, EELS analysis is performed, as demonstrated in Fig. 2 (a). The line-scan EELS spectra along the width of a single FCQD (4 M HF) is depicted in the inset of Fig. 2(a), where the characteristic peak located at 689.8 eV belonging to C-F bonding is monitored. By weighting the concentration of C-F bonding via estimating the integrated intensity of C-F signal, the line-scan tracing of C-F constitutes with relative to axial profiles of a FCQD is illustrated, as presented in Fig. 2(a). The results indicate that the F−-ion doping is preferentially located at separated positions around 0.5–1.5 nm and 5.2–3.8 nm, respectively, while the size of a FCQD is estimated to be around 5.8 nm. These findings reveal that the F−-ion constitutes only evenly exist close to the surfaces of FCQDs. In addition, the dependence of structural arrangement on doping concentration is revealed, as shown in Fig. 2(b). The pronounced XRD peak located at around 24 degree of Bragg angle can be clearly observed regardless of various F− doping from 2 M to 8 M of HF regents, which can be interpreted with the fact that the crystallographic graphite (002) patterns are retained from all synthesized FCQDs [2,9]. Specifically, the explicit red shift of diffraction angles with respect to the increase of concentration in doping agents is demonstrated. Although it remains puzzles to provide quantitative illustrations about the substantial change of surficial F− density, we may point out that the configurated expansion of graphite lattice of FCQDs as more F− ions reside within QDs under higher HF concentrations according to the concept of Bragg equation [9].

Spectral FTIR analysis of FCQDs prepared with various HF agents (2–8 M) are displayed in Fig. 2(c). Similar functional patterns, including C = C (1634 cm−1) and C = O (1721 cm−1) are assigned from all samples, which are originated from carbon-based features. Aside from that, the clear trends of C-F bonding (2989 cm−1) and CF-CH3 bonding (1263–1490 cm−1) showing the deeper adsorption patterns with the increase of F-ion doping reveal the fact that more F− ions are involved into QD structures. The structural crystallinity FCQDs are examined with Raman analysis, as shown in Fig. 2(d). We find that two characteristic Raman peaks assigned to D band (1417–1426 cm−1) and G band (1602 cm−1) are validated from all synthetic FCQD structures, while the correlated intensity ratio, in in terms of ID/IG, are distinct. The results indicate that the values of ID/IG are greatly reduced when F−-ion doping is adopted for the formation of CQDs in comparison with undoped CQD. This can be explained by virtue of compensation of originally-existing surficial defects, such as carbon voids, dangling bonds and others, with energetically favorable C-F bonding through the solution-based doping treatment presented here.

Finally, the photovoltaic performances of various FCQD-based solar cells are examined, as shown in Fig. 3 (a). The results indicate that the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of undoped CQD-involved solar cells is 6.8 %, where the limited photon-to-electricity conversion characteristic is mainly resulted from strong the charge-recombination due to relatively small potential barrier in between n-Si and undoped CQDs, which will be further addressed in Fig. 3(c). By contrast, the explicit improvement of all cell parameters, including open-circuit voltage (Voc), short-circuit current density (Jsc), fill factor (FF) and eventual PCE performances, are displayed from the FCQD/Si-based hybrid solar cells under various F−-ion doping treatments, as demonstrated in supplemental files. Foremost is the FCQDs synthesized with 4 M HF as doping regents, where the resulting PCE reaches 13.6 %, above two times of magnitude than that of undoped CQD cases. Fig. 3(b) reveals the current–voltage characteristics under dark condition by comparing doped FCQD (4 M HF)/Si and undoped CQD/Si samples. The dark currents at zero bias of doped and undoped cases are 1.28 × 10−10 and 5.96 × 10−9 A, respectively, and the reversing saturation currents are 1.03 × 10−9 A and 1.26 × 10−7 A, respectively. All these finding implicate that the doped FCQD/Si heterojunctions stand for the superior rectification capability than that of undoped CQD/Si heterojunctions, and in turn leads to suppress leakage currents in an efficient way. It should be noted that the excess F-doping on CQDs may distort the lattice configurations of C = C bonding features due to the mismatch of ion radius associated with compression stress surrounding F-ions, which negatively impact the photocurrent generation and active cell area for photoexcitation process. Accordingly, these thus reflect the reduced Jsc and FF of constructed solar cells when the concentrations of doping regents are higher than 6 M.

The band diagrams of both undoped CQD/Si and doped FCQD/Si heterostructures are presented in Fig. 3(c). Both of band features demonstrate the typical n-p diode junction established from the n-type Si in conjunction with p-type Si. The measured band-gap energy, conduction-band energy and valence-band energy of undoped CQDs and doped FCQD are fairly similar, which correlate with the nature of doped semiconductor materials. We notice that the Fermi energy of FCQD is comparably lower than that of CQDs, and this circumstance can be attributed to the electron-withdrawing effect of F−-ion involvement due to its high electronegativity [2]. Thus, the valence electrons of CQDs are grabbed by lattice F− ions, which lower down the eventual Fermi level to more negative value. This accounts for the relatively large barrier height (BI) at lower part of energy diagram when coupling FCQD with Si as heterostructures. Taking all, the circumstance of strong band bending at heterojunction due to reduced Fermi level of FCQDs elevates the efficient separation of photogenerated carrier under solar illuminations, which facilitates the photoexcited electrons moves to n-Si side and then collected with real electrode. Meanwhile, the photoexcited holes are injected into FCQDs and are then captured with graphene monolayer as hole collector, eventually contributing to the photocurrents.

In summary, FCQD (4 M HF)/Si hybrid solar cells with improved conversion efficiency of 13.6 % are demonstrated. This feature is elucidated with the superior charge separation under the circumstance of strong band bending at heterojunction, which paves ways for functional design of ion-doped QDs for practical applications.

Yu-Cheng Yen: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Po-Hsuan Hsiao: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation. Shih-Hsiu Chen: Visualization, Validation, Methodology. Chia-Yun Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Science and Technology Council (NSTC 110-2223-E-006 -003 -MY3).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2024.136359.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhancement of photoconversion efficiency of CdSe quantum dots sensitized Al doped ZnO/Si heterojunction device decorated with Ag nanostructures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Song, H. T. Nguyen, J. Park, T. T. Tran, M. Kumar, V. Bhatt, V. A. Dao, W. Lee, J. H. Yun, Enhancement of photoconversion efficiency of CdSe quantum dots sensitized Al doped ZnO/Si heterojunction device decorated with Ag nanostructures, Materials Science in Semiconductor Processing 149 (2022) 106878.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-sized carbon quantum dots enabling outstanding silicon-based photodetectors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.-H. Hsiao, Y.-C. Lai, C.-Y. Chen, Dual-sized carbon quantum dots enabling outstanding silicon-based photodetectors, Applied Surface Science. 542 (2021) 148705.

The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters

Yun, Photoluminescence properties of CdSe/ZnS quantum dot donor–acceptor via plasmon coupling of metal nanostructures and application on photovoltaic devices, The journal of physical chemistry letters 13(19) (2022) 4394-4401.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design of injection and recombination in quantum dot sensitized solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Barea, M. Shalom, S. Giménez, I. Hod, I. Mora-Seró, A. Zaban, J. Bisquert, Design of injection and recombination in quantum dot sensitized solar cells, Journal of the American Chemical Society. 132(19) (2010) 6834-6839.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Cho, J.A. Pan, H. Wu, X. Lan, I. Coropceanu, Y. Wang, W. Cho, E.A. Hill, J.S. Anderson, D.V. Talapin, Direct optical patterning of quantum dot light‐emitting diodes via in situ ligand exchange, Advanced Materials. 32(46) (2020) 2003805.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Kaur, M. Kaur, V.K. Sharma, Nitrogen-doped graphene and graphene quantum dots: A review onsynthesis and applications in energy, sensors and environment, Advances in colloid and interface science. 259 (2018) 44-64.
